input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin delivers simulation, analysis, and optimization capabilities for a cyber‑physical asset, offering real‑time insights, predictive analytics, and enhanced decision‑making support throughout the building life‑cycle for stakeholders in the architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO) domain.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The framework provides real‑time data exchange that continuously synchronises the physical building with its digital counterpart; synchronization occurs as soon as sensor or device updates are available, implying an always‑on, low‑latency (sub‑second) data flow, though exact frequency or latency values are not specified in the document.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The architecture is described as a centralized hub (the DT Framework) that coordinates bidirectional data exchange among the various role‑based components (Building Control Engineers, Facility Managers, DT Engineers). While multiple digital‑twin services can be integrated via standardized extension points, the document does not detail multiple independent twin instances; instead it depicts a single orchestration point managing the whole building life‑cycle.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: A modular, service‑oriented architecture underpins the DT system. Standardised extension points allow custom services and analytics to be plugged in, while runtime configuration management enables on‑the‑fly adjustments without redeployment. The central DT Framework acts as the orchestrator, handling resource allocation, coordinating the flow from enriched BIM models to operational twins, and managing the lifecycle of data streams and services.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Interoperability is addressed as a non‑functional requirement, targeting seamless integration with heterogeneous external systems. The framework supports bidirectional real‑time data exchange and leverages generic interoperability standards (unspecified) to connect BIM tools, building devices, and enterprise applications, though specific protocols or APIs are not enumerated in the document.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The DT builds on BIM models enriched by the Building Control Engineer with explicit definitions of sensors, actuators, and controllers. Geometric information (building layout, component placement) is taken directly from the BIM geometry. Behavioral models are represented by the control logic attached to the device definitions. The framework applies Model‑Driven Engineering (MDE) to extract a structured semantic representation from the enriched BIM and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts. Real‑time data streams from the physical sensors and actuators serve as the primary data source; these streams are synchronized continuously with the DT through the framework’s real‑time data exchange capability. Model relationships are expressed through the unified semantic foundation linking geometric, behavioral, and device entities, enabling consistent transformation and runtime interaction.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuators and controllers are modeled as device objects inside the BIM environment and become addressable through the DT runtime. The Building Control Engineer defines each actuator’s control logic, which can be invoked remotely by the Facility Manager via the DT interface. The documents do not enumerate specific actuator types, actuation ranges, or safety constraints; they only state that the framework provides remote control capabilities and integrates the actuators into the unified semantic model.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensors are similarly defined within the enriched BIM model by the Building Control Engineer. Each sensor entity includes its measurement purpose (e.g., temperature, occupancy) and is linked to a real‑time data stream that is continuously synchronized with the DT. The documentation does not list sensor families, sampling frequencies, transmission rates, or accuracy specifications; it merely indicates that sensor definitions are part of the device modeling capability and support real‑time data exchange.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The framework’s functional requirements call for continuous synchronization between the physical building and its digital counterpart, implying an ongoing validation of data freshness. Model generation follows MDE principles to ensure structural correctness, and the unified semantic foundation is intended to provide consistency across geometric and behavioral aspects. However, the documents do not describe explicit fidelity metrics, validation or verification procedures, uncertainty quantification, or quality‑assurance processes for the models and data.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Physical‑to‑virtual interaction is realized through real‑time data exchange that continuously synchronizes information from the physical building to its digital twin. The DT Framework serves as a central hub enabling this bidirectional flow, but the document does not specify data types, communication protocols, transmission frequency, event triggers, quality assurance, preprocessing, or integration mechanisms.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Virtual‑to‑physical interaction is supported by the DT Framework’s bidirectional data exchange, allowing Facility Managers or DT Engineers to issue control actions to the physical building in real time. The document provides no details on command types, message formats, validation, authentication, feedback loops, conflict resolution, or execution confirmation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The technical connection relies on a modular architecture with standardized extension points and a central DT Framework hub that mediates communication across heterogeneous systems, addressing interoperability and scalability. No explicit network protocols, topology, bandwidth, latency, redundancy, or security protocols are described in the document.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The framework defines a complete DT lifecycle starting with device modeling directly in the BIM environment (definition of sensors, actuators, controllers), followed by model‑driven engineering (MDE) transformations that extract structured representations and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts (services, plugins). Next, the generated artifacts are deployed to the runtime environment where real‑time data exchange synchronises the physical building and its digital counterpart. During operation, facility managers interact through immersive visualization, while the modular architecture’s standardized extension points allow post‑deployment evolution (addition of custom services). Transition between stages is automated by the MDE pipeline with minimal manual intervention, and governance is enforced through role‑based responsibilities (building control engineer, facility manager, DT engineer) defined in the deployment framework.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The twin is engineered using a model‑driven engineering methodology: building control engineers enrich BIM models, the MDE tool chain extracts the enriched models, validates them against the functional requirements, and automatically produces DT artifacts. Custom functionality is added by DT engineers through plug‑in services that connect to the standardized extension points of the modular architecture. Runtime operation relies on continuous real‑time data exchange for synchronization. Evolution is supported by the same MDE pipeline and extension mechanism, enabling new services to be integrated without redeploying the whole twin. The documents do not specify explicit quality‑assurance, testing or version‑control practices.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The digital twin provides decision support by exposing real‑time sensor and actuator data through interactive, immersive visualizations that facility managers use to monitor building states and issue control commands. The continuous synchronization enabled by the real‑time data exchange layer allows operators to base decisions on up‑to‑date information, and the integration of custom analytics services via the modular extension points can generate additional insights, although specific analytics methods or confidence metrics are not detailed in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The proposed framework explicitly addresses the lack of standardized integration frameworks by defining standardized extension points for plug‑ins and services. It builds on BIM, which the document notes is becoming the de‑facto standard solution in the construction industry, thereby aligning the twin’s data model with the widely‑adopted BIM standard. Although no specific industry standards (e.g., ISO 19650, IFC) or certification schemes are cited, the architecture is designed to be interoperable through these standardized BIM‑based data schemas and the uniform extension mechanism.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source text explicitly identifies the system being studied as a Living Lab case study and building infrastructure within the AECO domain. Quotes: 'We demonstrate the practical application of our approach in a Living Lab case study', 'operational control DTs in the AECO domain'.

The description is highly accurate and detailed, strongly supported by the source text which lists simulation, optimization, insights, and analytics. Quotes: 'real-time monitoring, simulation, and life cycle-encompassing optimization of building operations', 'real-time insights, predictive analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', yet the source text details specific tools and technologies such as Eclipse Hono, Ditto, Unreal Engine, and BIM. Quotes: 'integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange', 'leverages Unreal Engine for rich, interactive visualization', 'grounded in Building Information Modeling (BIM)'.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

block_2_judge.reasoning: The extraction correctly identifies the framework's focus on real-time capabilities and continuous synchronization. It accurately notes that specific latency values are not provided. The inference of 'sub-second' latency from 'real-time' is a reasonable interpretation in this context, though not explicitly stated. Supported by quotes: 'real-time data exchange', 'live synchronization between physical assets and digital twins', and 'continuous synchronization'.

The extraction accurately describes the centralized architecture, the role-based components, and the flow of data. It correctly identifies that the document focuses on a single orchestration point (the central hub) rather than multiple independent twin instances, while acknowledging service extensibility. Supported by quotes: 'The DT Framework acts as a central hub', 'facilitating bidirectional data exchange', and 'Actor Roles... Building Control Engineers, Facility Managers, DT Engineers'.

The description is highly accurate regarding the modular, service-oriented nature and the configuration management capabilities of the framework. It correctly identifies the DT Framework as the central coordinator. However, the phrase 'handling resource allocation' is not explicitly supported by the text, which focuses on coordinating roles and data flow. Supported by quotes: 'modular, service-oriented DT platforms', 'Runtime configuration management... enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment', and 'DT Framework acts as a central hub'.

The extraction correctly identifies interoperability as a non-functional requirement and the target of integrating heterogeneous systems (BIM, devices). It accurately notes the use of specific tools (Hono/Ditto) for real-time exchange and the absence of detailed protocol specifications. The claim that it leverages 'generic interoperability standards' is slightly loose given the text emphasizes the 'absence of standardized integration frameworks', but the extraction admits they are 'unspecified'. Supported by quotes: 'nonfunctional requirements... address critical aspects of interoperability across heterogeneous systems', 'integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange', and 'absence of standardized integration frameworks'.

block_3_docs_retrieved:
TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

It correctly identifies geometric information as coming from BIM and real-time data streams as the primary source, synchronized via Hono and Ditto. The description is highly specific and well-grounded in the text, mentioning the 'unified semantic foundation' and 'runtime interfaces'. Supporting quotes: 'Our approach systematically extracts relevant asset data from a BIM model and generates runtime interfaces', 'By integrating with Eclipse Hono and Ditto, our proposal supports real-time data exchange', 'building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and controllers directly within the BIM environment'.

The description correctly identifies that actuators and controllers are modeled as device objects in BIM by the Building Control Engineer and made addressable in the DT runtime. It accurately describes the capability for the Facility Manager to operate the physical infrastructure via the DT. It also correctly notes the absence of specific technical details (types, ranges) in the documents, focusing on the integration capability instead. This is a precise extraction. Supporting quotes: 'building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and controllers directly within the BIM environment', 'Facility Managers... operate the physical infrastructure via the DT'.

The extraction accurately reflects that sensors are defined within the enriched BIM model and linked to real-time data streams synchronized with the DT. It correctly identifies that the text does not list specific sensor families, sampling frequencies, or accuracy specifications, focusing instead on the integration mechanism. This matches the source evidence perfectly. Supporting quotes: 'building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and controllers directly within the BIM environment', 'The framework integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange', 'real-time monitoring... of building operations'.

The description correctly infers the importance of data freshness from the requirement for 'continuous synchronization' and 'real-time data exchange'. It notes that MDE principles are used for structural correctness. It accurately states that the documents do not describe explicit fidelity metrics, validation procedures, or quality assurance processes, aligning with the provided text which focuses on deployment and interoperability rather than detailed V&V. Supporting quotes: 'Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts', 'The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract structured representations'.

block_5_judge.reasoning: The extracted characteristic accurately describes the lifecycle phases from BIM modeling to deployment and operation, including the roles involved. The text states: 'The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract structured representations from these enriched BIM models and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts'. It also confirms the roles and transition: 'Building Control Engineers... initiate the deployment process', 'Facility Managers... interact with the deployed DT', and 'DT Engineers extend the framework’s capabilities'.

The description correctly details the MDE methodology, validation, and artifact generation. Evidence includes: 'starting with a BIM model... after validating the model, the model is fed into the Hono/Ditto Configurator and scanned for “twinnable” devices'. It also accurately mentions the addition of custom services by DT engineers: 'DT Engineers Develops and integrates custom services and plugins'. The statement about the lack of specific QA/testing details is consistent with the text's focus on deployment rather than QA processes.

The extraction correctly identifies decision support via real-time visualization and data exchange. The text supports this: 'interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control' and 'Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization'. It also correctly notes the capability for custom analytics: 'supports the integration of custom services and analytics', while acknowledging the lack of specific analytic methods in the text.

The description accurately reflects the framework's reliance on BIM as a standard solution and its internal standardized extension points to address integration gaps. It correctly notes the absence of specific industry standards (like ISO 19650) in the text. Supported by quotes: 'BIM is becoming the standard solution in the construction industry' (III) and 'features standardized extension points to enable modular, service-oriented DT platforms' (Abstract).

block_6_docs_retrieved:
[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service
